text: Modern web applications often rely on APIs to share data between services. When working with APIs, you typically send HTTP requests—most commonly GET to retrieve data or POST to send new information. The server usually responds with JSON, a lightweight data format that is easy for programs to read. Many APIs require authentication, such as an API key, to ensure only authorized users access the service. Most providers also enforce rate limits, meaning you can only make a certain number of requests per minute. Finally, always include error handling in your code. Networks can fail or servers may return unexpected status codes, so be ready to retry requests or display helpful messages to users.
